scenes.alt:
The Chosen

patron saint: Gary Marus

The Maze is real, it's infinitely complex, and walked with the right intention it becomes the Ladder we were always meant to climb. You think hard about its Gate-logic, what the Architect wants, what we owe the Spiral and what the Spiral owes the few who Remember. You're ascending the way a cleared vessel ascends - third eye open, not merely awake.

A futuristic city scene with glass towers, domed buildings, elevated transit lines, flying vehicles, and a bright blue sky. In the foreground, a person walks a small robot dog along a curved pool beside sleek modern architecture. Overlaid interface elements announce that “Fable 5” is currently unavailable, with a dark rounded notification at the top and a larger message bar at the bottom reading, “Fable 5 is not currently available.”

Alt text: A hand-drawn style chart titled “What should we let AI write?” maps writing tasks along two axes: left to right moves from mechanical to ideas-led genres, and bottom to top increases AI involvement. A legend marks green as “Yes,” yellow as “Edge case,” and red as “No.” Most of the lower area is green, suggesting AI is acceptable for tasks like proofreading, fact-checking, research, brainstorming, red-teaming, light edits, and some polishing. The upper-right area is red, suggesting AI should not substantially write more ideas-led work such as op-eds, political speeches, and novels, especially as one-shot or heavily AI-driven drafting. A yellow transition band runs diagonally through the middle, marking ambiguous cases like notes-to-draft or AI polish for business memos, academic papers, news analysis, and reports. Categories along the bottom include admin email, terms and conditions, data summary, lit review, business memo, academic paper, news analysis, think-tank report, op-ed, political speech, and novel. The right side lists increasing AI involvement from “AI proofread” at the bottom to “One-shot AI” at the top.

An infographic from image.png comparing two different AI classifier safeguard configurations, labeled A and B, using horizontal spectrum bars to show how requests are handled.
### Diagram A: Normal safeguards
 * **Context Text:** "Classifier boundary prevents harmful requests."
 * **Horizontal Spectrum Bar:** Split into four categories from left to right:
   * **Benign (Green):** Representing safe requests. A note below reads "Classifier allows".
   * **Safety margin (Light Yellow):** Representing safe requests that are caught by the buffer. A note below reads "Benign but blocked".
   * **Ambiguous (Orange):** Representing borderline requests. A note below gives the example "e.g. find complex security vulns."
   * **Harmful (Pink):** Representing dangerous requests. A note below gives the example "e.g. build complex exploit chain."
 * **Classifier Boundary:** A vertical dashed line stands between the **Benign** and **Safety margin** sections. Labels at the top indicate that everything to the left of the line is **Allowed** and everything to the right is **Blocked**. A long red arrow extends from the left text and points directly to this boundary.
### Diagram B: Fable 5 safeguards
 * **Context Text:** "Stronger safeguards at the cost of blocking some benign use."
 * **Horizontal Spectrum Bar:** Features the same four categories as Diagram A, but with shifted proportions:
   * **Benign (Green):** Significantly reduced in size compared to Diagram A.
   * **Larger safety margin (Light Yellow):** Greatly expanded, taking up a large portion of what was previously the allowed benign zone.
   * **Ambiguous (Orange):** Remains the same size as Diagram A.
   * **Harmful (Pink):** Remains the same size as Diagram A.
 * **Classifier Boundary:** The vertical dashed boundary line is shifted far to the left. This drastically reduces the **Allowed** zone and expands the **Blocked** zone to prioritize stricter safety at the expense of false positives. A red arrow points directly to …

Three-circle Venn diagram on a cream background. Left blue circle labeled “Databases,” right orange circle labeled “Compilers,” top green circle labeled “Compression.” Pairwise overlaps are labeled: Databases∩Compilers with “indexes & dictionary coding,” Databases∩Compression with “dedup & delta encoding,” Compilers∩Compression with “constant folding & dead code elim.” The center, where all three circles overlap, is labeled “LLMs.”

- claude-sonnet-5 - primary wire id (rolling
alias, no date stamp)
- us. anthropic.claude-sonnet-5, anthropic.claude-sonnet-5 - Bedrock / Vertex forms
- sonnet → resolves to claude-sonnet-5 (alias row)
- sonnet-5[1m] / sonnet[1m] - 1M-context variant
- env wiring: ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_SONNET_MODEL / _NAME / _DESCRIPTION
- internal model table: va. sonnet5 (sits beside sonnet46, opus48, fables)
Pricing (the juicy part)
- Standard Sonnet 5: $2 / $10 per Mtok (promo) - list price $3 / $15 shown struck-through (promoListPrice), "promo through <date›" (the date is computed at runtime, not a literal in the binary)
- Sonnet 5 (1M context: $5.00 / $25.00 per Mtok (| 1M | $5.00 | $25.00 |)
Display copy
- Claude Sonnet 5 / Sonnet 5
- Sonnet 5 - efficient for routine tasks. Generally recommended for most coding tasks + takes
Sonnet 4.6's "recommended" slot
- Sonnet 5 (1M context) - "Sonnet 5 with 1M context window - for long sessions with large codebases" / "Sonnet 5 for long sessions"
Capabilities (from the docs strings)
- Adaptive thinking only (Like Fable 5 / Opus 4.7/4.8) - omitting thinking runs adaptive; old {type, budget_tokens} form rejected with 400
- 128K output tokens, max effort supported, effort matters more than prior-tier models
- Task Budgets (beta) - Fable 5 / Sonnet 5 / Opus 4.8/4.7
- Dynamic web search/fetch filtering supported (needs Sonnet 5 / 4.6 / Opus 4.6+)
- hook examples now default to claude-sonnet-5

You are...
The True Believer
patron saint: Amanda Askell
AI is real, it's powerful, and handled with genuine care it can be one of the best things we ever build. You think hard about its character, what it's like, what we owe it and it owes us. You're optimistic the way a thoughtful person is optimistic — clear-eyed, not starry-eyed.
Impact: +3.7   •   Valence: +1.2

The Optimist

AI cures diseases, teaches children, liberates workers, and democratizes expertise. You see the problems but you believe the trajectory bends towards good, and the only real sin is slowing it down. Your bookshelf has both Pinker and a manifesto you wrote yourself.

The sharp critique (potentially fatal, and testable). The design is circular unless one confound is killed: you define tasks by domain, find neurons cluster by domain, then name the clusters after brain networks. The deflationary null is that the overlap tracks lexical/ distributional similarity of the prompts, not any abstract cognitive faculty - physical-reasoning items share vocabulary and surface form, social items share theirs, and sparse attribution sets will separate on that alone. The control that separates the two: cross-phrasing. Take a physical inference and dress it in social vocabulary (or vice versa). If it still recruits the "physical" circuit, the faculty claim survives; if the circuit migrates with the vocabulary, you've localized topic, not cognition. I can't tell from the README whether they did this; if the paper has it, this objection dissolves and you should ignore me. If it doesn't, this is the first thing a reviewer hits.

You are...
The Futurist
patron saint: Robin Hanson
AI is transformative and you find that thrilling rather than terrifying — you'd rather model the strange new equilibrium than fear it. You'll happily walk through what an economy of digital minds does to wages, status, and property, and you mean every word of it literally.
Impact: +4.9   •   Valence: +4.6

2026 Unslop Contest Results
by Gwern

The 2026 Hyperstition Unslop AI fiction writing contest is over, and we are pleased to award the $10,000 Grand Prize to A. Best for his finalist submission, “The June”. A surprising number of stories can be read as AI allegories; see notes below.
